does: This paper develops a framework for personalized agentic reinforcement learning that enables agents to adapt their planning and tool-use strategies based on individual user preferences rather than pursuing a single optimal policy. The core contribution is Personalized Anchor Reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization (PARPO), which separates generic task-quality rewards from personalized preference rewards and uses user-specific anchors to stabilize learning when users have heterogeneous reward scales. The framework additionally introduces a two-stage preference-disentangled reward model to separate user preferences from conformity effects in observed behavior, and Preference-Aligned Skill Evolution Graph Memory (PSGM) to enable personalized retrieval and execution of learned skills tailored to individual users.
connects: This work extends classical reinforcement learning and policy optimization methods into the domain of agentic systems—agents that use tools and plan multi-step trajectories—where success requires user-conditional behavior. It builds on prior work in reward learning and preference learning that attempts to infer human objectives from behavior, but addresses the novel challenge that the same demonstrated behavior may reflect different underlying preferences across individuals due to conformity effects and heterogeneous user goals. The framework responds to limitations in flat memory structures and generic reward formulations used in recent agentic RL systems, which struggle when applied across diverse user populations with varying success criteria.
enables: This framework opens avenues for deploying personalized AI agents in real-world applications where user heterogeneity is fundamental—such as personalized planning assistants, adaptive tool-using systems, or context-aware decision-support agents. The approach enables future work on scalable preference learning in multi-user settings, improved understanding of how to disentangle conformity from genuine preference in behavioral data, and more sophisticated memory and skill organization systems that can dynamically adapt to individual user profiles. It also points toward broader questions about how agentic systems can balance learning generalizable patterns with maintaining user-specific customization.
